model_findings.description: The chat completion schema accepts an unbounded list of messages with unbounded string/content-part sizes, and the endpoint immediately flattens that into memory before spawning an expensive backend CLI. A remote or accidentally misconfigured local client can submit very large bodies to consume memory, hit OS argv limits in the Gemini/Claude/Hermes paths, or tie up backend subprocess slots for up to the configured timeout. Add explicit body/message/content limits and reject oversize requests before flattening or invoking a backend.

The request model allows arbitrary extra OpenAI fields but the endpoint silently ignores fields such as stream, tools, tool_choice, response_format, temperature, max_tokens, and stop. This is particularly problematic for stream=true or tool-use clients, which will believe they requested OpenAI-compatible behavior but receive a normal non-streaming text completion with no indication that requested semantics were dropped. Unsupported fields should either be implemented or rejected with a clear 400 error.

The Gemini, Claude, and Hermes backends pass the full user prompt as a command-line argument. Prompts often contain secrets or private data, and command-line arguments are visible to local process inspection tools while the backend runs; large prompts can also fail with argument-list limits. Prefer stdin or a temporary file with restrictive permissions for all backends, as the Codex path already does for input.

The CLI exposes a --host option that is passed directly to uvicorn while the proxy has no authentication, authorization, quota control, or request-size limits. If a user binds to 0.0.0.0 or another non-loopback address, any reachable client can spend the user's configured model credentials and drive local agent CLIs. For local-only tooling, reject non-loopback hosts by default or require an explicit unsafe flag plus authentication.

The new item-level gate does not enforce the canonical error-correction authoring schema: it only requires `sentence` and `error`, treats `answer`, `options`, and `explanation` as optional, omits the schema-supported `error_type` field, and allows `correction` even though the JSON schema/quick-ref define `answer` as the canonical corrected-form field. As a result, valid writer output containing `error_type` will be rejected, while incomplete or non-canonical output such as an item with only `sentence`, `error`, and `correction` will pass this gate despite violating the documented schema contract.
